task.title: Vending Machine: Dispense Product
task.description: The dispenseProduct() method body is missing. Implement it to fetch a product by ID, verify it is in stock, decrement the quantity, persist the update, and return the correct change. Reject out-of-stock products with OutOfStockException and insufficient payment with InsufficientFundsException.
constraints: Throw OutOfStockException when the product is not found or quantity <= 0
Throw InsufficientFundsException when amountPaid < product price
Decrement quantity AND persist via productRepository.save(product) — an in-memory decrement alone is a lost update
Change returned must equal amountPaid - price

senioritySignals: Checks stock before payment — fail-cheap ordering avoids charging for an unavailable product
Calls productRepository.save(product) after decrementing — not just an in-memory update
Derives change as amountPaid - price rather than a hardcoded or unrelated value
Notices that change is only returned/printed and never reconciled against a CashRepository
Considers @Transactional or optimistic locking for the read-modify-write on quantity
commonMistakes: Decrements quantity in memory but forgets to call productRepository.save() — lost update
Checks payment before stock — charges the buyer for a product that was already out of stock
Hardcodes change or returns amountPaid instead of amountPaid - price
No atomicity around the read-modify-write of quantity under concurrent dispenses

correctness.9-10: All paths handled: both exceptions thrown correctly, stock persisted via save(), change is amountPaid - price
correctness.7-8: Happy path + exceptions present; one minor gap (e.g. missing save() or incorrect exception ordering)
correctness.4-6: Basic dispense works but misses 1-2 constraints (e.g. no save, wrong exception type)
correctness.1-3: Fundamentally wrong or incomplete — missing exception handling or wrong return

efficiency.9-10: O(1) single findById + single save; candidate names the complexity and mentions concurrent-update safety
efficiency.7-8: Correct but one unnecessary extra fetch or no complexity discussion
efficiency.4-6: Works but naive (e.g. reloads all products to find by ID)
efficiency.1-3: Ignores efficiency / introduces an obvious anti-pattern

interviewerFocusAreas.scope: Ordering of stock vs payment checks — does it matter which exception is thrown first? Walk through the case where payment arrives for an already-empty slot and the candidate checks payment before stock.

Read-modify-write on Product.quantity when two buyers hit the same last unit simultaneously — what happens with the naive findById + decrement + save pattern, and how would @Transactional or an atomic UPDATE fix it?
Change is computed but never reconciled against a CashRepository — what would a correct money-flow look like, and how would you model exact-change availability?

expectedApproaches.tradeoff: Simple and O(1); not safe under concurrent dispenses without @Transactional or version-based optimistic locking

expectedApproaches.approach: Atomic conditional UPDATE (UPDATE products SET quantity = quantity - 1 WHERE id = ? AND quantity > 0)
expectedApproaches.tradeoff: Concurrency-safe at the DB layer without application-level locking; slightly harder to read and requires custom @Query in the repository

knownEdgeCases: Product ID not found in repository
quantity == 0 exactly at purchase time (not just negative)
amountPaid exactly equals price — zero change, must still succeed
Two concurrent dispenses of the last unit in stock
scaleUpDimensions: Multiple vending machines sharing one inventory store — distributed locking or DB-level atomicity required
Exact-change availability tracked in CashRepository — dispense should fail if machine cannot make change

followUpIntent.hint: If the candidate did a plain read-decrement-save without @Transactional, probe the concurrent last-unit race condition. If they handled stock cleanly with locking, probe the missing cash/change reconciliation against CashRepository.
